edges.description: Posttraumatic dissociative organization is modeled as a proximal contributor to dissociation and dissociative self-states.
Dissociative compartmentalization is represented upstream of gaps in recall and dissociative amnesia.
Early trauma-linked dissociative organization is modeled upstream of hippocampal correlates of dissociative amnesia.
edges.target: Hippocampal Subfield Abnormality in Dissociative Amnesia

edges.description: Posttraumatic developmental mechanisms are modeled upstream of cortical morphology differences associated with dissociative symptoms.
Posttraumatic developmental mechanisms are modeled upstream of temporal and orbitofrontal surface-area differences associated with dissociative symptoms.
CA1 and hippocampal volume differences are modeled as neurostructural correlates of dissociative amnesia.
edges.source: Hippocampal Subfield Abnormality in Dissociative Amnesia

edges.description: Cortical morphology differences are modeled as neuroanatomical correlates of dissociative symptoms.
Temporal and orbitofrontal surface-area reductions are modeled as neuroanatomical correlates of dissociative symptoms.

nodes.description: Dissociation and dissociative self-states are core clinical features.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of dissociative identity disorder: leveraging neurobiology to optimize success.
evidence.snippet: In this narrative review, the authors discuss symptom characteristics of DID, including dissociative self-states.

nodes.description: Structural MRI evidence links DID and dissociative amnesia to smaller hippocampal volumes, especially bilateral CA1 subfields.
nodes.id: Hippocampal Subfield Abnormality in Dissociative Amnesia

evidence.reference_title: A neurostructural biomarker of dissociative amnesia: a hippocampal study in dissociative identity disorder
evidence.snippet: Hippocampal volumes were found to be smaller in DID as compared with HC in bilateral global hippocampus and bilateral CA1, right CA4, right GC-ML-DG, and left presubiculum.

evidence.reference_title: A neurostructural biomarker of dissociative amnesia: a hippocampal study in dissociative identity disorder
evidence.snippet: Dissociative amnesia was the only dissociative symptom that correlated uniquely and significantly with reduced bilateral hippocampal CA1 subfield volumes.

nodes.description: DID is associated with decreased cortical thickness in the insula, anterior cingulate, and parietal regions, with associations to dissociative symptoms and early childhood traumatization.

evidence.reference_title: Neurodevelopmental origins of abnormal cortical morphology in dissociative identity disorder
evidence.snippet: Individuals with DID differed from controls in CV, CT, and SA, with significantly decreased CT in the insula, anterior cingulate, and parietal regions and reduced cortical SA in temporal and orbitofrontal cortices.

evidence.reference_title: Neurodevelopmental origins of abnormal cortical morphology in dissociative identity disorder
evidence.snippet: Significant negative associations between abnormal brain morphology (SA and CV) and dissociative symptoms and early childhood traumatization (0 and 3 years of age) were found.

nodes.description: Dissociative amnesia causes clinically important gaps in recall.

evidence.reference_title: A neurostructural biomarker of dissociative amnesia: a hippocampal study in dissociative identity disorder
evidence.snippet: Little is known about the neural correlates of dissociative amnesia, a transdiagnostic symptom mostly present in the dissociative disorders and core characteristic of dissociative identity disorder (DID).

nodes.description: DID treatment is represented as psychotherapy-centered care informed by dissociative symptoms, safety/stabilization needs, and trauma-processing readiness.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of dissociative identity disorder: leveraging neurobiology to optimize success.
evidence.snippet: Current treatment approaches are described, focusing on empirically supported psychotherapeutic interventions for DID and pharmacological agents targeting dissociative symptoms in other conditions.

evidence.reference_title: Separating Fact from Fiction: An Empirical Examination of Six Myths About Dissociative Identity Disorder
evidence.snippet: Accurate diagnoses are critical for appropriate treatment planning.

evidence.reference_title: Separating Fact from Fiction: An Empirical Examination of Six Myths About Dissociative Identity Disorder
evidence.snippet: If DID is not targeted in treatment, it does not appear to resolve.

nodes.description: DID is represented as a complex posttraumatic developmental disorder in which dissociation becomes organized into persistent self-states and clinically important discontinuities in memory and self-experience.

evidence.reference_title: Separating Fact from Fiction: An Empirical Examination of Six Myths About Dissociative Identity Disorder
evidence.snippet: Dissociative identity disorder (DID) is a complex, posttraumatic, developmental disorder for which we now, after four decades of research, have an authoritative research base, but a number of misconceptualizations and myths about the disorder remain, compromising both patient care and research.

nodes.description: DID is associated with reduced cortical surface area in temporal and orbitofrontal cortices, represented separately from cortical-thickness findings because cortical thickness and surface area have distinct developmental origins.

evidence.reference_title: Neurodevelopmental origins of abnormal cortical morphology in dissociative identity disorder
evidence.snippet: Individuals with DID differed from controls in CV, CT, and SA, with significantly decreased CT in the insula, anterior cingulate, and parietal regions and reduced cortical SA in temporal and orbitofrontal cortices.

evidence.reference_title: Neurodevelopmental origins of abnormal cortical morphology in dissociative identity disorder
evidence.snippet: As CT and SA have distinct genetic and developmental origins, our findings may indicate that different neurobiological mechanisms and environmental factors impact on cortical morphology in DID, such as early childhood traumatization.
